day1.Description: Mindy Price, Kendall Gow, and Terri Crowell — This session sets the stage for 2026, connects where we have been to where we are going, and lands the theme that carries through everything that follows: Supervisors bring the weather. Come ready to reflect, reconnect, and set your intentions for the next three days.

day1.SessionTitle: Bringing the Weather: Setting the Tone for Leadership
day1.Description: Frank Martz, City Manager — Frank has a direct conversation about what leadership requires, what this city expects, and what it means to show up as a force multiplier for your team every single day. Your people feel your weather before you say a word. This is a reminder of the weight that comes with that, and a clear picture of the standard at the City of Altamonte Springs.

Alex Desrosiers, Fisher Phillips — The legal environment supervisors operate in is not static. This session covers what has shifted, where liability lives when supervisors act without information, and how to make decisions that protect both the people on your team and the City.

Mindy Price — This is the heart of Day One. Cross-functional groups rotate through hands-on stations, each one built around a different dimension of leadership and what it takes to create a climate where people can do their best work. No two groups start in the same place. Every group ends in the same one.

Mindy Price — Some stations are physical. Some will bring you out of your comfort zone. All of them will tell you something about how you lead, how your team functions, and what kind of climate you are creating. The day closes with every group naming their climate.
Mindy Price, Kendall Gow, and Terri Crowell — A close that connects what happened today to what comes next.

day2Supervisor.SessionTitle: AI Tools for the Everyday Supervisor
day2Supervisor.Description: Robert McDaniel — AI is already in your workplace. This session skips the hype and goes straight to what is actually useful: the tools worth your time, the prompts that get real results, and how to stay in the driver's seat when AI is doing the grunt work. You will leave with things you can open before the week is out.

day2Supervisor.SessionTitle: Strengths in Action: How Your Team Sees You
day2Supervisor.Description: Jaia Bright — Most supervisors do not hear honest feedback about how they land. This session creates the conditions for that. You'll paint. No rules, no artistic expectations, and nothing you are required to share. Quieter and slower than most sessions. More lasting than most sessions.

day2Supervisor.SessionTitle: Safety Culture: The Climate You Create
day2Supervisor.Description: Traci Krewson — Safety is not a checklist. It is what happens when the climate a supervisor creates makes it easier to speak up than to stay quiet. This workshop examines how day-to-day supervisor behavior determines whether a team operates proactively or reactively around risk.

day2Supervisor.SessionTitle: Leading Across Generations (Without Losing Your Mind)
day2Supervisor.Description: Kim Maldanado, Neida Hernandez, Evelyn Steenekamp, and Dean Fathelbab — A younger supervisor managing someone who has been at the City longer than they have been alive. An experienced supervisor managing someone with completely different expectations about feedback and pace. Both situations are real. Both are getting more common. This session focuses on what actually works when the gap is that wide.

day2Supervisor.SessionTitle: What Happens When Your Expert Leaves?
day2Supervisor.Description: Glen Cobb — Every department has someone who knows things no one else knows. This session is about what you do before that person walks out the door, and what it takes to build a team where knowledge moves across people rather than staying locked in one of them.

day2Supervisor.SessionTitle: After the Conversation: Leading with Clarity, Not Comfort
day2Supervisor.Description: Todd Smith — Having the hard conversation is one challenge. The day after is a different one. This session is for supervisors who want to know what to do once the difficult moment has passed: how to hold the line, stay consistent, and lead someone who knows exactly what you said and is watching to see if you meant it.

day2Senior.Description: Mindy Price — The day opens with an experience that makes visible something most leadership teams have felt but rarely named: what happens when a goal lives clearly at the top but travels through the organization without its meaning intact.

Mindy Price — Directors and deputy leaders go deeper into the dynamics of their specific layer. How do the daily decisions about time, communication, and visibility either reinforce or quietly undermine the climate below? This session asks leaders to look honestly at which of their behaviors they would want to replicate down the chain and which ones they would not.

Mindy Price — Band 3Ds engage in the same hands-on activity as Bands 1 and 2. What happens when the layer closest to the front line is in the seat? How do the daily decisions about time, communication, and visibility either reinforce or quietly undermine the climate below?

Mindy Price — The day closes with all three bands working through the structure they will carry into Day Three with their divisions. The focus is practical: level of authority, how to use time, how to direct resources, and how to facilitate the Day Three conversation in a way that keeps the intent of the two days before it alive.

day3.SessionTitle: Weathering the Storm: A Survival Guide for Everyday Life
day3.Description: Amy Sweezey — A keynote that uses weather as a true lens, not a metaphor, for what it takes to navigate difficulty, reinvention, and the kind of uncertainty that does not resolve neatly. Amy Sweezey opens Day Three by asking what the supervisors in this room have already survived to get here and what that means for where they go next.

Mindy Price and Terri Crowell — Each division operates like its own weather system. It has its own pressure patterns, its own flow of resources, its own internal dynamics. But no division exists in isolation. All of your ecosystems are part of the Altamonte Springs Ecosystem. This session brings divisions together to understand how your individual ecosystems function, and how they interact with the City as a whole. What conditions are you creating internally through your people, tools, and time?
Ryan Thompson — What makes this organization distinct is the culture. This session examines the culture Altamonte Springs is actively building and asks supervisors to name their own role in it.

Mindy Price — A conversation within each group about who they serve, how they contribute to the city that exists beyond their department, and what climate they are responsible for creating. The forecast your team built over three days becomes visible all at once.
Kim Maldanado — Conversations, light snacks, and the people who run this city, together.
Mindy Price, Kendall Gow, and Terri Crowell — A close that honors what the three days produced and names what comes next.
